lines: An old woman in salt-stained robes. She does not stand when you arrive.
'I am the emissary of Karst-by-the-Sea. We sent grain. Mournhold returned it sealed.'
'My queen closed our gates after that. Not in anger. In economy of grief.'
'We have been watching the Pall reach our border. It has not crossed yet. It will.'
'I am here to ask you one question. The answer will not change what we do.'

responses.text: Why are you here if it won't change anything?

lines: 'When you reach the Summit and break the Warden — what do you choose?'
'If you let the Pall keep you, Karst stays closed. We have decided.'
'If you refuse, we open. Slowly. With a long winter to teach the merchants how not to forget.'
'I am asking which you intend, so we can ready the gates either way.'
'Because I have grandchildren in Karst. They asked me to ask. They are afraid.'
'They have heard the Pall move at night. They wanted me to come and SEE who would break it.'
'They are children. I am not going to tell them you said anything I wouldn't want them to know.'
'But you should know that they sent me. The grandchildren of the merchants who sent the grain.'
'Then we will open. Slowly. The bread will move both ways across the border again.'
'It will take a generation. Karst has long memory. But the gates will open.'
'Thank you. My grandchildren will be glad. They will not know to be glad of you specifically.'
'I will not tell them your name. You will not be in our histories. That is the price we charge.'
'Then we stay closed. I will tell the children. They will be afraid. I am afraid for them.'
'You are choosing the kingdom that returned our grain sealed. That is your right.'
'I do not curse you. I do not have time. Go to the Summit. We will be here, watching.'
She nods. She has seen indecision before.
'Then go and decide. Come back if you wish to talk again. I am here for some time.'
'Karst can wait. We have waited three winters. We can wait the time it takes you to choose.'
A man in muddy practical armour. He is digging a small grave. He looks up.
'I am here unofficially. Wynne does not admit any of this happened.'
'We sent two thousand. The Pall took them. The Pall took the Captain who led them.'
'She is still here, walking the slope. She still believes she is on a relief march.'
'I am here to bury what I can find of the two thousand. I find about six a year.'
'I have a request. It is also unofficial.'
'Put the Captain down. She does not deserve to keep walking.'
'She is not the Pall's. She is ours. She believed in us. We failed her.'
'If you can lay her to rest — without the Pall taking what is left of her —'
'I will tell Wynne, off-record. Wynne will open its gates to Karst, off-record.'
'A chain begins. None of it will be on the official ledgers. It will still be a chain.'
'Because two thousand families would learn how their soldiers died.'
'And those families would burn Wynne's chancellery to the ground.'
'And rightly. Our Chancellor knew the Pall was contagious before the army crossed.'
'He sent them anyway. He thought a victory would distract from a famine he caused.'
'I cannot fix any of that. I can bury what I find. Will you help.'
'Yes. They should. They will not. That is not what we are here to discuss.'
'I am here to bury soldiers. You are here to choose what to break. Can you help me bury one.'
'Or can you not. I will keep digging either way.'

responses.text: I'll help. I'll put her down.

lines: 'Thank you. She is up the slope. She is waving her sword at trees.'
'When you put her down, do it kindly. She believed she was relieving a starving people.'
'She still does, somewhere. The Pall is over that part of her too thinly to drown the belief.'
'After. Come back. I will have something for you to carry back to Karst, and to Mournhold both.'
'Another day, then. I will be here. The graves are slow work.'
A small clean man in a clean cloak. He smiles when you arrive. Genuinely.
'Welcome to the Margrave's border-monument! It is open to all visitors. Refreshments inside.'
'My master sends his regards. He has been very moved by the Mournhold tragedy.'
'He authored the relief-camp model that fed three thousand refugees in the second winter.'
'For a modest fee, of course, but at a competitive rate compared to outright starvation.'
'How may I welcome you on the Margrave's behalf today?'

responses.text: Why did your Censor try to kill me?

lines: His smile does not waver. It does not have to.
'A regrettable but predictable consequence of the relief-camp model.'
'Refugees without funds cannot be accommodated. The Margrave is not a charity.'
'Their names are removed from the camp ledger upon expiration. This keeps our records clean.'
'The Censor handles removal. We find clean records improve donor confidence considerably.'
'A tenth of any assets in the camp at the time of arrival.'
'For a refugee from Mournhold, this typically meant: a wedding ring. A boot. A child's tooth-charm.'
'These items go to the Margrave's collection. He keeps them in a display case at the residence.'
'It is a moving display. I have visited it. I recommend it sincerely.'
He looks surprised. He shouldn't have. He cleans his cuff.
'The Censor is empowered to remove all evidence of unprofitable visitors from the monument.'
'You are not unprofitable, of course. We have nothing here to gain from you. So our apologies.'
'If you would like to be added to the monument's record of distinguished visitors —'
'For a fee — we can ensure your name appears on the relief plaque, posthumously or otherwise.'
He nods. Cleanly. The smile becomes thinner. Not absent. Just thinner.
'A philosophical disagreement is the Margrave's prerogative to receive.'
'You may inform Karst and Wynne, if you wish, that you have lodged opposition.'
'It will not change the relief-camp model. It will, however, embarrass us in their newsletters.'
'That has its own value. I will note your name as having declined the plaque arrangement.'
'Thank you for visiting. Please do not return without a fee.'
A woman in her forties. Hard-eyed, not angry. She has counted you before you got close.
'I am Tálva. My great-grandmother sat on Mournhold's council and voted to open the gates.'
'They sealed her in a room for it. She wrote my line of the family on the wall before she died.'
'We came out of the holds Mournhold let starve. We have been here three generations.'
'I have a request. I will not pretend it is a kindness to you.'

responses.text: What do you want from Mournhold?

lines: 'There is a shrine on the Climb. The first king's vow. The seal under the mountain.'
'I want it broken. Not repaired — broken. I want the hunger to take Mournhold's name.'
'I want them unremembered, the way they unremembered our holds.'
'You can climb. You can reach it. If you break the shrine, the kingdom that ate ours will be ate.'
'I will not lie and call it justice. It is symmetry. Symmetry is what we have left.'

responses.text: I won't help you do this.

lines: 'Children were passed under the gates the night before they sealed.'
'The mothers stayed inside. They knew there wouldn't be enough grain for the children too.'
'My great-grandmother's daughter — she was three weeks old — was one of the passed children.'
'She was raised by a Karst trader who happened to be near the gate that night.'
'There are forty-three of us, descended from that night. There were more. Mournhold's Pall has
'caught some of us, even out here. We do not forgive that.'
'I want them to know what they did. They cannot know if they cannot remember.'
'I want the kingdom unmade. I want their name forgotten the way they forgot ours.'
'I do not want apologies. I do not want their gold. I do not want their grain.'
'I want symmetry.'

responses.text: And what becomes of the people of Mournhold?

lines: She does not answer for a long time.
'The same as my great-grandmother. The same as my great-aunts. The same as the seventy-six',
'people in our family ledger who died inside the sealed gates without their names being said.'
'It is not a kind answer. I am not the kind one here. Kerris is. Ask him if you want kindness.'
'We do live. Three generations of us have lived. That is not the question.'
'The question is: what do we owe the kingdom that did this. The answer is: nothing.'
'The further question is: what does the kingdom owe us. The answer is: itself.'
'I am collecting on that debt.'
She nods. She does not press.
'Go up. Speak with the others before you decide. Kerris and Ondrek and the Small Un.'
'Then come back. I will not be hurt if you refuse. I am not waiting for you to decide.'
'I will find another climber if you walk away. There are climbers every year.'
She nods again. She is harder to surprise.
'Then go. Tell them, when you climb the road back, that we are here.'
'Tell them that we remember. Tell them that some of us would have unmade them.'
'It will not undo the gates. But Mournhold should know that the gates were never the end.'
An old man at a small clay oven. He bakes one loaf a day, no more.
'I am Kerris. My grandmother was one of the children passed under the gates.'
'I keep her oven. I bake the way she taught me — one loaf, every day, no exceptions.'
'She made me promise. The bread she taught me how to bake was the bread her mother taught her.'
'It is the last bread that was baked in Brackmere before the gates closed.'
'I have one loaf today. I have kept one back, every day, for three years.'
'I do not know who I was keeping them for. I think it might be you.'

responses.text: What do you think of Tálva's plan?

lines: 'Mira. She was three weeks old when the gates closed.'
'The Karst woman who carried her out called her Mira because it was the only name she liked.'
'When my grandmother was old enough to ask, she went back to Brackmere and looked.'
'There was no Brackmere. The records had been struck through. The hold was a flooded field.'
'She came home and bought the oven. She baked. She taught my mother. My mother taught me.'
'I bake every day. It is enough. It is what I was given.'

responses.text: What do you think of Tálva's plan?

lines: He wraps the loaf in cloth. The cloth is older than he is.
'Take it. Eat it when something needs eating that gold cannot fix.'
'It is not a magic bread. It is just bread. But it is bread that the gates did not stop.'
'That counts for something. Some day, it will count for you.'
He kneads dough for a long while before answering.
'I love Tálva. She is my cousin. She is right that Mournhold owes us a debt.'
'But I bake. I do not unbake. I have not lifted my hand to anyone in thirty years.'
'If you help her, do it for Mournhold, not for us. We do not need it done.'
'We have bread. We have the oven. We have my grandmother's name. That is enough.'
'Tell her, if you decide. Tell her gently. She has been carrying this since she was small.'
A man in his thirties, sharpening a knife with too much attention.
He looks up.
'You are Mournhold-born. I am not. I have one question. I want a real answer.'
'Do you think Tálva is right?'
He nods. Slowly.
'Good. I am not sure either. I have been working through it for ten years.'
'I voted against Tálva's plan, the first time we met to discuss it. Eight years ago.'
'I voted against it the second time. Six years ago.'
'I have not been to the last three meetings. I think that means I have changed my vote.'
'But I cannot make myself say so out loud, to Tálva or to anyone.'
'That is what I wanted to tell you. Knowing what is right and being able to say it are different.'
'Whatever you decide, climb up and say it. Out loud. We owe each other at least that much.'
He nods. He sharpens the knife.
'Tálva will be glad. She will not say she is glad. But she will be.'
'You are doing something I have not been able to do for ten years. Good for you.'
'I am not sure it is right. I am sure it is loud. Make it loud.'
He looks at the knife. He puts it down. He looks at you.
'I think you are wrong. I also think I have not been able to say that out loud.'
'You said it out loud. So I will too.'
'I think Tálva is right. I am going to her tonight. To tell her.'
'Thank you. I needed someone else to refuse first. I needed to know it would be said.'
A child. Perhaps seven years old. They are eating something — a slice of bread.
They look at you with the absolute, total attention of small children.
'You are from up there.' They point up, toward Mournhold.
'Tálva says you are going to break it. Mama says you might not.'
'Mama says nobody knows.'
They consider. They keep eating.
'I want the bread to keep coming. I want Mama not to be angry all the time.'
'I want Tálva not to be sad.'
'I do not know if Mournhold being broken would do those.'
'I do not know what Mournhold is, very much. I have been there in dreams.'
'In dreams it is the place where you have to be afraid to be a child.'
'I would not want to live there. But I do not want it broken, either.'
'Mama is Yara. She is the one who bakes the bread when Kerris is too old to bake.'
'She is also the one who has to listen to Tálva when Tálva is being too much.'
'Mama says Tálva is right but Tálva is too loud.'
'Mama says you can be right and too loud at the same time.'
'Tálva said that you are going to climb the mountain and break a shrine.'
'And then the kingdom will go away. I am supposed to be happy about that.'
'I am trying to be happy. It is hard. The kingdom never did anything to me — Tálva did not let it.'
'I do not know what it is like to be done something to by a kingdom.'
'I do not know if I should learn or not.'
I am Cael.
I was the daughter of the first king of Mournhold.
There was a kingdom here before Mournhold. It was called Verren.
Something ate Verren. We were the ones who came after.
My father wrote the vow above me. We will eat last. We will say the names.
He kept it. After him, it weakened. Now it is forgotten.
I am the seal. I have been the seal for a long time.

responses.text: Tell her about Atrél, in the Choir's south aisle.
Tell her you found her father's pommel — Garren, first of the holds.
Tell her you read down the column of seals behind her.
Tell her what the Pall wrote in the rite-book.

lines: You tell her. You read it to her. She does not interrupt.
When you are done, the stone where her mouth is grinds against itself.
She is, in some way, beginning to weep without tears. Stone weeps.
It is a slow, almost geological motion. You wait. She is taking time.
'I did not know,' she says. 'I have been the seal between us for —'
'(she does not finish the year.)'
'I have been the wall between the kingdom and what came out of the rite.'
'I have not known what came out of the rite. I held it. I have not heard it.'
'I would not have heard it through stone, even if it had spoken.'
'It said: I did not ask for this.'
'It said: I would like to lie down.'
'It said the same things I have been saying. Quietly. Under stone.'
'I have been quiet because the stone made me quiet. It has been quiet'
'because there is no one above to hear it. We have been quiet at each'
'other for centuries. We have been the same quiet. I did not know.'
'Thank you for telling me. I am — for the first time in I do not know how'
'long — less alone in here. I have something to think about. I will think.'
'Go climb if you are going to climb. I will be here. I will be — somewhere'
'in this stone — thinking about a tired thing the size of a kingdom.'
'I have been one of them. I did not know I had a sibling. I am sorry I did not.'
You read down. Good.
I did not know all of them. I knew Kess. Kess was my older sister.
She came down for what we both thought was Verren-hunger renewed.
She was wrong about the renewed part. The hunger was already there.
She held it for sixteen winters. Then she was done. Then I came down.
I carved her name above mine when I sat down. The chisel was already there.
It is always there. The first seal left it. We have all used the same chisel.
We sharpen it. We re-sharpen it. The handle is the smoothest thing in this room.
I did not know Thene. Thene was Kess's predecessor. Kess carved Thene above her own.
I knew the name. I knew nothing about her. That is the way the line tends to work.
Each of us knows the one who came right before us, sometimes. Each of us knows
the one who comes after, sometimes. The rest is just the names.
The Unnamed — the one whose marks stop — I do not know what happened. Mar carved Iria.
Mar did not carve the Unnamed. The Unnamed was supposed to carve Mar, and did not finish.
I think the Unnamed lost her sense of language somewhere in her years of holding.
She wrote Mar's first letter and then could not remember letters anymore.
Mar sat down anyway. Mar did not need a name to seat herself. Mar did the work.
The line is older than language. The line outlasts knowing the language.
If you read all the way down to NO-NAME — yes. That was the first.
We do not have her name. We have only the absence of one. The first seal did not
name herself. She left no name to carve. I think she meant it. I think she meant
the line to be a thing that did not need naming. She thought we would not need to count.
She was wrong. We need to count. We need the names.
I have done my best with that. I am sorry, NO-NAME.
I have carved KESS and you will carve KESS and CAEL — both — for me, when you sit.

responses.text: I have not said I will sit yet.

lines: Garren.
It has been a long time since anyone has said his name aloud here.
I have been saying it, but I am the seal, and the seal does not count as aloud.
He was a hold-keeper before he was a king. He told me that, the night
before I came down. He said: 'Cael. The kingdom we built is a hold
made larger. Do not let them forget it was a hold first.'
He had a pommel he carried — brass, worn smooth, from his father's sword.
He set it into the altar of the shrine the day he wrote the vow.
He said: 'I am setting this down so that whoever comes after, if they
kneel low enough, will see I was small once. Kings should be reminded.'
You found it. He thought you would. He always thought a stranger would.
Tell him, when you sit down with the climbers who come after you:
tell them his name was Garren, and that he was a hold-keeper.
Tell them his daughter has been saying his name under the mountain
for longer than the kingdom has had words for the years. She is still saying it.
Atrél.
I have not heard that name in centuries.
He held the small forgettings. I held the older one.
My father came down here on the night before I descended.
He sat at my feet and said three hundred names and then he said:
'I am going to set one more thing down at Atrél's altar before I climb.'
'It is that I have a daughter, and I am about to ask her to do this.'
I did not know, until now, that he did. Tell me what he looks like.
(You tell her. A small old man whose work was taken away from him.)
She is quiet for a long time. The stone she has become
is moving very slightly, around her open mouth.
'Tell him,' Cael says, 'that I have been saying the names,'
'and that one of them, lately, has been his.'
'Tell him I will say his quietly until the seal is done.'
'Tell him: the daughter remembers. The kingdom forgot.'
'The daughter did not forget.'
A hunger under the mountain. Older than language.
It does not eat bodies. It eats the memory of bodies — and then,
after a generation has passed and the memory is gone,
the bodies that the memory belonged to also stop having ever existed.
Verren forgot itself like that. So thoroughly.
No archive of theirs survived. Only the word VERREN, and only because
my father carved it into the stone of the shrine before he started forgetting.
The Pall is not new. The Pall is the old hunger.
What the rite of unremembering did was simple. It cracked the seal I am.
It let some of the hunger out, dressed as a thing the kingdom could explain.
But there is no Pall above. There is only hunger. There has only ever been hunger.
Above ground it wears the colour grey. Below ground, where I sit, it has no colour.
It only has a mouth. The mouth is mine. The mouth is the seal.

responses.text: How long have you been here?

lines: Because somebody had to be. My father was an old man. I was twenty-one.
The hunger was already loud under the mountain when Mournhold was founded.
We knew it would devour us as it devoured Verren, unless something held.
I offered. I sat down here. I said the names of Verren as I knew them
until the stone closed over my mouth and I could only say them inside.
I am still saying them. Quietly. Quietly.

responses.text: Can someone else take your place?

lines: Verren names. Hold-names. The two villages that were here before Verren.
I learned them all from my father, the night before I came down.
He sat at my feet and named them, slowly. Both my parents did. Three hundred names.
I have not stopped saying them. Some I have forgotten the spelling of.
I do not let myself forget the sound. The sound is the seal. The sound is the seal.

responses.text: Can someone else take your place?

lines: I do not know. The stone does not let me count winters.
Long enough for the kingdom above me to forget I existed.
Long enough for them to invent another rite — the rite of unremembering —
and use it on their own gates instead of on the hunger below.
Long enough to be very tired. Long enough to want to lie down.
But the seal is the mouth, and the mouth still has names to say.

responses.text: Can someone else take your place?

lines: Yes. Someone with a mouth, and a long memory, and a willingness to stop walking.
It would have to be permanent. It would have to be silent.
You would not climb back up. You would not be remembered above.
The Pall above would die because the hunger below has its mouth back.
Mournhold would live. Mournhold would not know how. They would not know your name.
But the seal would hold for as long as you said names. Centuries. As I have.
I would lie down. I would finally lie down. It would be a gift.
Thank you. Thank you.
I will teach you the names. We have time. The stone is patient.
When you have learned them, climb back up — only once more —
and break the Warden. After the Warden falls, come down again.
Sit where I have sat. The stone will know what to do. So will you.
Thank you. Thank you. Thank you.
Then go. I have held this long. I will hold longer.
Maybe forever. The stone does not seem to be in a hurry to release me.
If you change your mind, the stair is still here. The seat is still here.
Go and break the Warden. Climb back, or do not. Either is yours to choose.
Say my name to no one. It would not help anything. Thank you for asking.
The figure at the altar is small. Smaller than the altar.
He does not look like what you expected a god to look like.
He looks like an old man whose work has been taken away from him.
'I am Atrél,' he says. 'You found the marker. You found the registrar's book.'
'You found the side-altar where they tried to burn me out, and could not.'
'So you know.'

responses.text: Tell him about Cael, under the mountain.

lines: Atrél does not look surprised that you read the folio. He has been
waiting for someone to. He sits a little straighter against the altar.
'Renaud was a steward,' he says. 'He kept the rites of the cathedral.'
'He brought small forgettings here for many people. He was good at it.'
'He was tender. He cried, the first time he brought a grief here for a widow.'
'I remember that. I held the grief. He sat with me, after, for a long while.'
'When the famine winter came, he was afraid. He had seen people starve before —'
'his uncle, in his hold, when he was small. He had been carried out of that hold'
'with the people who would not, themselves, eat that winter. He owed those people.'
'He thought: if we just forget this one winter, we will not break under it.'
'He thought: the rite is a kindness. I will make the kindness bigger.'
'He read my folio. He saw the page that said: one person, one loss.'
'He took the page out. He told himself that was a small change.'
'He told himself my altar was a place of compromise, anyway, and that compromise'
'was what people needed to survive. He told himself this several times.'
'You have to tell yourself a thing several times before you can do it.'
'I do not hate him. He was a man who loved his people enough to do a terrible thing.'
'That is the kind of person who does the terrible things, in the end. The cruel'
'ones rarely have the courage. It is the kind ones who scale a kindness past its size.'

responses.text: Did he know what he was doing?
Would he have done it again, given the choice?

lines: Atrél nods slowly. He does not look away from you.
'He knew. That is the part the kingdom does not tell.'
'The night before the rite, he came here. He sat at this altar. He could'
'not bring himself to ask me to forget what he was about to do. He knew it'
'would not have worked. He sat for an hour. He cried, the way he cried'
'the first time, when he brought the widow's grief.'
'Then he went up to the cathedral. Then he did it.'
'He knew he would be unwritten by his own rite. He knew no one would ever'
'say his name again. He did it anyway, because he thought it was the only'
'kindness left to do at the scale he had built it to. He was wrong. He was'
'also brave. Brave and wrong is the worst combination a kind person can be.'

responses.text: Would he have done it again?

lines: Atrél thinks about this for a long while.
'I do not know,' he says. 'In the moment — yes. He thought he was saving'
'his people. He would have done the moment again.'
'But if you gave him the moment and the rest of his life back, after, to live'
'with what he had done — knowing he would be unwritten, knowing the wound'
'would grow into the Pall, knowing a small god he had loved would be eaten'
'from inside by it for centuries — I do not know if he would have done it then.'
'No one ever gets the moment and the rest of their life back. He had to choose'
'with only the moment in front of him. He chose. That is what a person is.'
'If you climb the Summit and say the names, you can say his — if you want to.'
'I would rather you said the small ones he was kind to. The widow he brought here.'
'The boy whose first cruelty he set down here. Those are the people he was kind to.'
'The terrible thing was not all of him. It just outlasted the rest.'
Atrél listens. He does not speak for a long time.
When he does, it is quieter than before, the way one old keeper
speaks of another old keeper they have heard of, but never met.
'She has been holding the hunger,' he says. 'I have been holding the griefs.'
'They are the same problem at different scales. Memory you cannot stop.'
'I knew the first king. He kept the vow above her. He kept it for me, too.'
'He used to come here, before he climbed back up to teach her the names.'
'He set down a grief at this altar — that he had a daughter, and he was'
'about to ask her to do the hardest thing his line had ever asked.'
'I took it. He climbed back up lighter for it.'
'Tell her, when you go back down to her: a small god remembers her father,'
'and remembers that his last grief was her. Tell her her father did not,'
'in the end, forget that.'
'For small forgettings,' he says. 'A slap given in anger. A child's first cruelty.'
'A husband's last word that should not have been the last word.'
'Things people brought me, set down at this altar, and walked away lighter for.'
'You cannot carry every hurt. You should not have to. That is what I was for.'
'I was a small god. I was the right size for the work.'
'I taught it. Slowly. To the keeper of this side-altar, first.'
'Then to her daughter. Then to a circle. Then to the cathedral above us, when it was built.'
'They wrote it down. They taught it back to me, with corrections.'
'I let them. I was proud. A small god gets proud of small things.'
'When they came to me with the kingdom-rite — I should have refused. I did not refuse.'
'I told them: you are asking the cup to hold the sea. They said: we will be careful.'
'They were not careful. The cup broke.'
'They took my rite. They scaled it past me.'
'They did not ask me to forget a hurt. They asked me to forget a winter.'
'They asked me to forget the gates closing on the holds, and the holds going quiet,'
'and the children at the gates, and the order from the capital that no help would be sent.'
'I could not hold it. No god my size could have held it. They knew that.'
'I broke. What broke out of me — that is what walks the Climb now. That is the Pall.'
'The Pall is not a thing I made. It is what was left of me, after they took too much.'
'The famine winter,' he says, and looks down at his hands for a long while.
'The famine winter is the wound the rite was scaled to cover. The wound is still there.'
'You walk over it every time you climb the road. The Pall is the wound's voice now.'
'A wound speaks until it is heard. I did not get to hear it. I was made to forget it instead.'
'I am still here because gods do not die quickly. We diminish.'
'There is a little of me left. A little of the rite I used to be.'
'If you bring my rite back to me — not at the kingdom-scale, only at the altar-scale —'
'I can take it back. The Pall is what slipped out of me when they over-asked.'
'If I take the rite back at my size, the Pall has nothing to be made of, and it ends.'
'It will end softly. Not loudly. Not the way you have been told it would end.'
'You will not be remembered as the one who broke it. You will not be remembered at all.'
'I cannot offer you a story. Only an end.'

responses.text: I'll bring the rite back to you.
I want the story. I'll do it the loud way.

lines: 'It can. There are two ways I know of.'
'One: you climb the Summit and say the names back, every one, and the Pall stops because',
'it has been heard. This is the way the world has been built to give you. Mournhold purified.'
'You will be remembered as the one who said the names.'
'Two: you bring the rite back to me. At my altar. At my size.'
'The Pall does not stop because it is heard. It stops because it is no longer being made.'
'You will not be remembered. The kingdom will go on, not knowing how close it came.'
'It is the smaller ending. It is also the only ending where I get to die in peace.'
'Because gods are made by the stories we tell about them.'
'If you take the loud ending, the story keeps me dead. The world has decided I never existed.'
'If you take the small ending, the story does not know about you. But it knows about me.'
'Someone will set a small grief down at this altar again, one day, and walk away lighter.'
'That is the difference. Pick the ending. Both are kindnesses. Neither is for you.'

responses.text: I want the kingdom to remember me.

lines: He nods. He does not look hurt. Gods that small have learned not to look hurt.
'Then go,' he says. 'Climb. Say the names. Be remembered.'
'I will be here. Diminishing.'
'If you change your mind on the way up, you can come back. The road runs both ways.'
Atrél closes his eyes. He looks, for the first time, almost rested.
'When you break the Warden,' he says, 'do not say the names yet.'
'Come back here. Set the rite down where I can take it.'
'I will end. The Pall will end with me. No one will know either of us were ever here.'
'It is the only thing I have left to offer you. Thank you for accepting it.'
